criteria.label: Choose a dict over a list whenever access is by key.
criteria.example: Looking up a doc by ID? `docs_by_id[doc_id]` (O(1)) beats `next(d for d in docs if d.id == doc_id)` (O(n)). If you're ever writing a `for` loop to find one matching item, you probably want a dict.

criteria.label: Recall that core dict operations are O(1) average, O(n) worst case.
criteria.example: `d[k]`, `d[k] = v`, `k in d`, `del d[k]` are all O(1) on average. Worst case O(n) happens only with pathological hash collisions (e.g. attacker-controlled keys) — extremely rare in normal use.

criteria.label: Identify which keys are hashable (str/int/tuple) vs not (list/dict/set).
criteria.example: `d[('user', 42)] = ...` ✓ (tuple of immutables → hashable). `d[['user', 42]] = ...` → `TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'`. Hashability needs immutability.

criteria.label: Use get / setdefault / defaultdict / Counter idiomatically.
criteria.example:
```python
# count tokens per doc
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
count = Counter(tokens)               # cleanest
by_doc = defaultdict(list)
for d in docs: by_doc[d.lang].append(d)
x = d.get('retries', 0)               # safe lookup with default
```

criteria.label: Build a dict-by-id index to convert O(n²) list scans into O(n).
criteria.example: Joining two lists by `id`? Don't nest loops (O(n*m)). Build `idx = {x.id: x for x in xs}` once (O(n)), then `[idx[y.id] for y in ys if y.id in idx]` (O(m)) — total O(n+m).
